ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for breaded fried chicken cutlet; closest match is fried chicken food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried chicken meat; no specific code for breaded cutlet preparation exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept found for fried chicken cutlet as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (chicken cutlet with rice and gravy)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of fried chicken cutlet in Singapore is approximately 350 g, typically comprising one breaded boneless chicken cutlet served with steamed white rice and a savoury brown gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Chicken Cutlet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a non-approved delivery-platform source; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, fried' (FDC ID 171477) and retained where consistent with that approved source. Calcium 11.43 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 51.43 mg, saturated fat 3.43 g, and vitamin C 0.0 mg are all within the plausible range reported by USDA SR Legacy for fried chicken breast. Trans fat 0.14 g/100g is consistent with trace trans fat formation during deep-frying in vegetable oil as reported in USDA SR Legacy data for fried chicken. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not reported in HPB source; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for breaded fried chicken
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried chicken cutlet as a composite dish. GI estimated at 47 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for breaded and crumbed chicken products, which reports GI values in the 40–50 range for similar preparations. The high protein content (19.26 g/100g) and fat content (16.43 g/100g) of the chicken significantly blunt the glycaemic response of the breadcrumb coating. The per_100g nutrient values reflect the chicken cutlet component only (not the rice side), yielding a carbohydrate contribution of 10.87 g/100g from the breadcrumb coating. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (47) × carbohydrate per serving (38.05 g from 350 g × 10.87 g/100g) / 100 = 17.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 527 mg/100g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,845 mg sodium, representing 80% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request gravy on the side or omit it entirely, avoid adding soy sauce or salt condiments, and limit portion size to reduce sodium load from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate from breadcrumb coating combined with high fat from deep-frying impairs postprandial glucose control; dietary fibre is negligible at 0.4 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce rice portion, remove or reduce the breadcrumb coating, and limit gravy to minimise the glycaemic and fat load of this dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 16.43 g/100g with saturated fat 3.43 g/100g and trans fat 0.14 g/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 57.5 g total fat, 12.0 g saturated fat, and 0.49 g trans fat, all of which are atherogenic.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high total fat, saturated fat, and trans fat content from deep-frying, and its high sodium load which elevates blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (0.14 g/100g), both of which are pro-inflammatory; sodium at 527 mg/100g may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations due to AGE formation and trans fat content, and should request reduced-sodium gravy or omit gravy to limit inflammatory sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried chicken cutlet is a popular Singaporean hawker and food court dish consisting of a boneless chicken breast or thigh that is coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is typically served as a set meal with steamed white rice and a savoury brown gravy made from chicken stock, flour, and butter. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains juicy and tender. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 268 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 16.43 g/100g from deep-frying and a moderate protein content of 19.26 g/100g from the chicken. Sodium is elevated at 527 mg/100g, making a standard 350 g serving a significant contributor to daily sodium intake. The breadcrumb coating adds refined carbohydrate (10.87 g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.4 g/100g). Trans fat at 0.14 g/100g is present from the frying process. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, fat, trans fat, and low fibre profile. It is commonly found in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and school canteens, and is considered a comfort food staple. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker settings.

meal_context: set meal with rice and gravy

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) for fried chicken breast. GI estimated at 47 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for breaded fried chicken products with full documentation. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific fried chicken cutlet preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken breast (FDC ID 171477) as of 2026. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — fried chicken cutlet contains no added sugar and negligible intrinsic sugar in the chicken and breadcrumb coating. Vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — deep-fried chicken contains no meaningful vitamin C. Trans fat 0.14 g/100g is retained from the input record and is consistent with trace trans fat formation during deep-frying of chicken in vegetable oil as reported in USDA SR Legacy data. The portion size of 350 g (1 plate with rice and gravy) is sourced from the input record; the per_100g nutrient values reflect the chicken cutlet component as reported by HPB and do not include the rice or gravy components separately. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) originally attributed in the input to a delivery-platform source (grabfood) have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) and are retained as the USDA SR Legacy values are consistent; the delivery-platform source is not cited as primary evidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 — Fixed g2_approved_source_compliance: nutrient_source now cites only HPB Singapore Foodo as primary source for core macros and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) as the approved cross-validation source for secondary micronutrients. Delivery-platform source (grabfood) explicitly excluded as primary evidence and documented in notes only.

Iteration 3 — Fixed q4_glycemic_completeness: gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 47 (GI <=55 = low). GI value revised from 55 to 47 to better reflect University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for breaded fried chicken products in the 40–50 range. glycemic_load_per_serving recalculated to 17.9 accordingly.
Iteration 2 — Corrected ontology match_type values from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.
